matches.match_rationale: Campus rectors resist change partly because mandates from HQ feel generic and disconnected from their specific context. AI can generate campus-tailored versions of best-practice change communications — framing the same initiative through the lens of each campus's geography, student demographics, and resource profile — reducing the 'this doesn't apply to us' reflex. This directly attacks the narrative mechanism of NIH resistance.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An AI system that takes a network-wide best-practice rollout and auto-generates campus-specific change briefs, talking points, and FAQ responses customized to each site's known context — so rectors receive a message that already acknowledges their campus's constraints.

matches.match_rationale: A key driver of adoption resistance is the perceived time burden of reading and understanding lengthy research or case studies from other campuses. AI summarization can condense evidence packs — including outcome data from pilot campuses — into brief, scannable TLDRs tailored for time-poor campus leaders, lowering the cognitive cost of engaging with external evidence.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An AI-assisted evidence digest tool that auto-summarizes pilot results, research backing, and implementation guides from other campuses into a 1-page brief per initiative, delivered to rectors before each rollout decision.

matches.match_rationale: Rectors often claim their campus is unique to avoid adopting practices — but this claim frequently goes unchallenged because no one can quickly retrieve precedents from other campuses that share similar constraints. A RAG system over the network's internal knowledge base (campus profiles, past pilots, outcome data) lets HQ staff or rectors themselves ask 'which campuses with similar demographics ran this and what happened?' — grounding the conversation in evidence rather than assertion.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A network knowledge assistant that lets HQ coordinators and campus leaders query internal pilot records, outcome reports, and campus profiles in natural language — surfacing evidence-backed responses that counter uniqueness claims with comparable precedents.

matches.match_rationale: Adoption resistance often persists because rectors never get a low-stakes space to voice objections and receive tailored responses. A conversational AI agent can serve as an always-available 'change readiness coach' — letting campus leaders explore objections, ask 'what if my campus tries X instead?', and receive contextually grounded answers drawing on network data. This surfaces resistance early and responds to it at scale without requiring HQ staff bandwidth.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A conversational change-support agent embedded in the network's intranet or LMS that campus leaders interact with when preparing for a new initiative — guiding them through readiness questions, handling objections with evidence, and escalating genuine blockers to HQ coordinators.

matches.match_rationale: When rectors submit feedback or responses to proposed initiatives, HQ coordinators often lack the bandwidth to triage which objections reflect genuine campus-specific constraints versus generic NIH resistance. AI text classification can categorize incoming objection text by type — legitimate constraint, resource concern, cultural resistance, or procedural pushback — allowing coordinators to prioritize and respond appropriately at network scale.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An objection triage layer on the network's initiative feedback system that classifies rector responses by objection type, flagging legitimate constraint claims for human review and routing resistance-pattern responses to a standardized evidence-based reply workflow.

matches.match_rationale: Not all campuses resist equally — some rectors are chronic resisters, some are early adopters, and some are swing cases that respond to the right framing. A propensity model trained on past rollout outcomes, rector engagement history, and campus profile features could predict which campuses are likely to resist a specific initiative, enabling HQ to allocate change management effort proactively rather than reactively.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A campus adoption propensity score — updated per initiative — that predicts likelihood of resistance based on historical engagement patterns and campus characteristics, helping network coordinators decide where to invest pre-launch relationship-building versus where a standard brief suffices.
